primary_diagnosis.name: Exposure keratopathy secondary to mild Graves' ophthalmopathy (lid retraction); corneal ulcer/microbial keratitis not excludable remotely

comorbidities.name: Graves' ophthalmopathy (thyroid eye disease) with lid retraction

pathophysiology_summary: Graves' ophthalmopathy causes lid retraction and reduced blink/closure, exposing the inferior cornea and predisposing to exposure keratopathy — a diffuse superficial punctate epitheliopathy causing intense gritty/burning surface pain, photophobia and reflex tearing, especially with a drying draft (fan) and lapsed lubrication. It is treated with aggressive lubrication and protecting the ocular surface. However, a severe painful photophobic red eye overlaps with sight-threatening emergencies — microbial/herpetic keratitis and corneal ulcer — that require slit-lamp and fluorescein assessment to exclude; acute angle-closure glaucoma is also screened. Telehealth cannot perform these, so the correct action is interim lubrication plus urgent (same/next-day) ophthalmic assessment, and NOT prescribing topical anaesthetic for home use.

differential.evidence_basis: Gritty/burning surface pain with photophobia and tearing triggered by a draft, on a background of thyroid eye disease with lapsed lubrication.
A painful photophobic red eye mandates excluding an ulcer/keratitis; screen for discharge, corneal opacity, lens use, visual loss.
No injury/particulate history; cannot evert lids remotely.
Screen for deep ache, haloes, nausea, mid-dilated pupil; absent here.

No purulent discharge/corneal opacity/lens use/dense visual loss (DI-001), draft-triggered surface pain with exposure risk (DI-002), no angle-closure features (DI-003) — exposure keratopathy is most likely, pending slit-lamp confirmation.
Cannot be excluded without fluorescein/slit-lamp; retained as the escalation trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: copious lubricating ointment, protect the surface (tape/cover, avoid the fan), urgent same/next-day optometry/ophthalmology, and a vision-loss/pus safety-net; explicitly do NOT prescribe home topical anaesthetic. ED for angle-closure/vision loss; routine review would risk a missed ulcer.

red_flags.description: Corneal ulcer/microbial keratitis — purulent discharge, corneal white spot/opacity, contact-lens use, or significant visual loss
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sight-threatening corneal infection needing urgent ophthalmic care.

red_flags.description: Acute angle-closure glaucoma — deep ache, haloes, nausea/vomiting, mid-dilated pupil
red_flags.clinical_significance: Emergency causing rapid irreversible vision loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Assuming exposure keratopathy and reassuring without excluding an ulcer/keratitis by slit-lamp.
Topical anaesthetic drops for home use impair corneal healing and mask worsening — a recognised harm.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Lid eversion for a foreign body
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Copious lubricating ointment + surface protection; urgent same/next-day optometry/ophthalmology; ED for angle-closure/vision loss.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Assuming exposure keratopathy and prescribing anaesthetic drops could miss a corneal ulcer and cause vision loss.
